name: 50 · Legacy File Crawl + Inventory Register (file-ops crawl stream → register)
description:
End-to-end demo of the file-ops `crawl` operation and the by-reference inventory register path (docs/32 — the legacy file-migration capability). Nothing touches a real NAS: the first step SEEDS a small synthetic file tree on local disk, then the workflow crawls it, folds the streamed listing, and registers each physical copy into `file_inventory` by reference (no bytes moved).

  Start{file_server_id, mekhan_url} → seed_nas (write ~7 files into /tmp/mekhan-crawl-demo) → crawl (file-ops `crawl`: recursive streaming walk, emits `{path,size,mtime}` batches over the `crawl` control channel) → fold (consumer-edge `join: gather` flattens the batches) → register (POST the rows to `/api/v1/inventory/register`) → End.

The `crawl` op is `list`'s recursive, streaming, checkpointable sibling, built for the ~4M-file legacy corpus: it never buffers the whole listing, `stat()`s each entry for size+mtime (the OpenDAL `fs` lister omits them), and emits fixed-size `item` batches over the job EventStream — the same docs/25 consumer-join mechanism the streaming demos use. Here `batch_size: 3`, so the ~7 seeded files arrive as multiple batches; the `crawl → fold` edge carries `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders and parks the whole collection as `{output: [<batch>, …]}`, which `fold` flattens to a flat `{path,size}` list. The `register` step stamps each row with the Start `file_server_id`, sets status `indexed` (no content hash yet — hashing is the later `probe` step's job), and POSTs them to mekhan's own inventory API; the rows then show up in the app's Inventory view. Because crawl carries no hash, `catalogue_inserted` is 0 — content-addressed catalogue rows only appear once a `probe` populates `content_hash`.

Run it against your dev stack: `mekhan run demos/50-legacy-crawl-register --server http://localhost:13100 --input start.mekhan_url=http://localhost:13100 --input start.file_server_id=demo-nas` (point `--server` and `start.mekhan_url` at your slot's mekhan port — e.g. 20100 for slot 1). It is self-contained (stdlib only, no pip installs) so it completes well within the `mekhan test` cap, but the register step needs to reach mekhan over HTTP, so the executor must be co-located with the seeded directory and able to reach the service URL — true for the default single-host `just dev` stack.
